title: Foundational hand, the basic strokes
subtitle: The seven strokes that build every letter
excerpt: The seven basic strokes that underlie Edward Johnston's Foundational hand, the calligraphy alphabet first taught in his 1906 Writing & Illuminating & Lettering. Learn the strokes in isolation before any letter; the alphabet then assembles itself from the practised parts.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing & Illuminating & Lettering (1906), § The Foundational Hand (Project Gutenberg #34879): the seven basic strokes, the 30° nib angle, the 4 nib-width x-height, and the stroke-order conventions that distinguish Foundational from earlier rounded hands. The exemplar plates in Plates 4 and 6 of the 1906 edition show the strokes in the order given here. Johnston is the canonical English public-domain source for Foundational.

recipeTools.notes: Mid-range Mitchell Roundhand broad-edge nib. The default for Foundational practice at roughly 4 nib-widths x-height. A Brause Bandzug or Speedball C2 of comparable width works equivalently.
Plain wooden pen holder; the broad-edge nib drops into it. The oblique holder used for Copperplate is wrong for Foundational.
Modern non-corrosive iron-gall ink, or any well-flowing carbon-based dip-pen ink. Avoid fountain-pen ink for broad-edge work; it dries patchy.
130 gsm cartridge, substantial enough to take a wet nib without buckling, light enough to take guideline rulings without ghosting.
Helpful for ruling guidelines without indenting through to the next sheet, slide a piece of card underneath.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat-cut dip-pen nib (as opposed to the pointed nib used for Copperplate). The width of the cut determines the thickness of the broadest stroke. Held at a constant angle to the baseline, the nib produces thick-and-thin strokes through changes of direction, not pressure.

The angle between the flat-cut edge of a broad-edge nib and the baseline (the horizontal line letters sit on). Foundational uses a 30° angle held constant for every stroke; Italic uses 45°; Roman capitals use 0° for verticals.
The vertical distance from the baseline to the top of a lower-case letter without an ascender (such as the letter x). Measured in nib-widths; Foundational practice uses 4 nib-widths.

The order and direction in which the strokes of a letter are made. A letter's ductus is part of its definition, drawing the strokes in the wrong order produces a letter that looks subtly wrong even when the shape is right.

content.text: Foundational hand is the entry-point calligraphy script: upright letters, simple strokes, even spacing. The goal at this stage is consistency, not flourish. A clean Foundational hand is the base every other Western broad-edge script builds on (Italic, Uncial, Carolingian all derive from the same strokes).

Edward Johnston's Foundational hand, published in his 1906 Writing & Illuminating & Lettering, was the calligraphy alphabet that recovered the broad-edge tradition for the twentieth century. Based on the round-form Carolingian minuscules of the tenth century, with their generous proportions and clear stroke shapes, Foundational is the alphabet every modern Western calligraphy course teaches first. The reason: every other Western broad-edge hand (Italic, Uncial, Carolingian, Versals) builds on the same set of basic strokes, drawn at a constant
nib angle
against a fixed
x-height
. Practise the strokes; the alphabet follows.

Seven strokes do the work. A vertical downstroke, a horizontal across the top of the letter, the two diagonals (one for the right-leg of an n, one for the left-leg of an a), a curve forming half a circle, the dot that appears on the i and j, and the connecting hairline of an h or m. Each stroke is drawn from start to finish in a single confident movement; the nib stays at 30° to the baseline throughout. The thickness of the stroke comes from the nib's angle relative to the direction of the stroke, not from pressure on the pen.
Setting up, paper, guidelines, nib angle

Cut or tear a sheet of 130 gsm cartridge into A5 practice sheets. On the first sheet, rule guidelines: a baseline (where the letters sit), an x-height line above it, an ascender line above that, and a descender line below the baseline. The vertical spacing comes from the nib width, hold the nib flat against the paper at 30° and step its width up the page four times from the baseline. That four-nib-width measurement is the x-height. Ascender and descender lines sit a further three nib-widths above and below.

Rule the guidelines with a soft pencil and a ruler, very lightly. The lines should be visible but not press through to the next sheet. The bone folder behind the ruler helps keep the pencil from indenting.
The
broad-edge nib

is held at 30° to the baseline through every stroke. Pick up the pen and check the angle by laying the nib's full width flat against the page, the flat edge of the nib should sit at the 30° line. The angle stays constant; you do not rotate the pen during a stroke. Hold the pen with the grip relaxed enough that the nib glides across the paper without dragging.
The seven basic strokes
Each stroke below has its
ductus

, the direction the nib travels. Practise each stroke five times across the width of the practice sheet before moving to the next. The strokes should sit on the baseline, reach the x-height line for the body strokes, and reach the ascender or descender for the long strokes. Even thickness through the body of the stroke; thin where the stroke direction meets the 30° nib angle head-on; thick where it meets the angle at 90°.
1. The vertical downstroke

The vertical stem that runs from the ascender or x-height line down to the baseline. Starts at the top with the nib's full width meeting the line; ends at the baseline with the nib's full width touching down. The body of the stroke is the thickest the nib can lay down, perpendicular to the 30° angle. Found in the stems of b, d, h, k, l, p, q, m, n, i, j (and in capital I, T, F, H stems where Foundational caps are used).
2. The horizontal stroke

Runs left to right at the x-height line (or the ascender line for capitals). The stroke is at the thin end of the nib's range, the 30° nib meeting a horizontal stroke means the nib's edge is nearly aligned with the stroke direction. Found in the crossbars of t and f, the top of capital T, the central stroke of e and B.
3. The right-leaning diagonal

Top-left to bottom-right. The 30° nib angle aligns closely with this stroke direction, so the stroke reads as a medium-thick line, not the thickest the nib can lay. Found in the diagonals of v, w, x, y, z, and in capital V, W, X, Y, Z.
4. The left-leaning diagonal

Top-right to bottom-left. The 30° nib meets this direction at close to a right angle, so it reads thick, the heaviest stroke the nib produces. Found in the upper-left strokes of v, w, x, y, z, and in capital V, W, X, Y, Z.
5. The bowl curve

Half a circle, top to bottom, opening to the right. Starts thin at the top (the nib meeting the curve head-on), thickens through the middle as the curve direction rotates against the 30° angle, ends thin at the bottom. Found in the bowls of b, d, p, q (and reversed in the bowl of o, e, c). The shape is the heart of Foundational, round, generous, evenly weighted.
6. The dot

A short vertical mark on top of the i, j, and the cross of t. The dot sits between the x-height and ascender lines. Drawn as a very short downstroke of the nib at full width, then a clean lift. Not a circle, not a square; the natural footprint of the nib at 30° to the baseline.
7. The connecting hairline

The thin curve connecting the top of one vertical stem to the top of the next, found in h, m, n, the second stroke of u. The stroke moves the nib upward and over, ending at the second stem. The whole stroke runs at the thin end of the nib's range; the curve should not thicken in the middle.
Practising, twenty minutes of strokes before any letter

Practise each stroke five times across a row of guidelines, then rule a second row and do five of the next stroke, and so on through the seven. Twenty minutes total. The first row of each stroke is the rough draft; the fifth is usually the one that reads as a clean Foundational stroke. Don't move on to letters until each stroke can be drawn five times in a row at consistent thickness and shape, the letters depend on the strokes, and a rushed stroke produces a wobbly letter no amount of touch-up corrects.

The next tutorial in this sequence works the seven strokes into the lower-case Foundational alphabet, n, o, h, a, e, i, l, t, then the rest of the letters by combination. The strokes themselves don't change; the letters are arrangements of the seven shapes you practise here.
If the strokes go sideways

attrs.heading: Common first-strokes problems
attrs.intro: Five things that go wrong on first Foundational practice and the fix for each.

items.symptom: Strokes are thick in the wrong place, the verticals look thin and the horizontals look thick
items.cause: Nib angle held at 60° rather than 30° (the most common beginner error, the natural held angle of a pen is closer to 60°).
items.fix: Lay the nib flat against the baseline and physically rotate the pen until the flat edge sits at 30°. Hold that angle through every stroke without rotating. A small 30° wedge of paper taped to the corner of the desk gives a constant visual reference.

items.symptom: Strokes are uneven thickness through the body, gaps and thin patches
items.cause: Nib not flat against the paper; one corner of the nib is lifted, so only part of the cut edge is laying ink down.
items.fix: Press the nib firmly enough that both corners of the cut edge touch the paper. The pen is held in the fingers, not gripped; the weight of the hand provides the pressure.

items.symptom: Ink flow stops mid-stroke, the nib runs dry halfway across
items.cause: Insufficient ink loaded, or the nib's protective coating wasn't removed before use.
items.fix: Re-load the nib by dipping just the underside (the lower side of the cut) into the ink up to the breather hole. Don't dip the whole nib, that floods the holder. If the nib is new and has never inked cleanly, wipe it with a cloth and try again; if still patchy, pass it briefly through a flame (1-2 seconds) to burn off the manufacturing oil.

items.symptom: Strokes wobble, straight lines don't come out straight
items.cause: Drawing the stroke from the wrist or fingers rather than the forearm; the wrist is the wobble source.
items.fix: Lock the wrist and draw the stroke from the elbow / shoulder. For verticals: anchor the heel of the hand on the paper, pivot the forearm down. For horizontals: anchor and pivot the forearm right. The wrist stays still.

items.symptom: Ink puddles or blots at the start or end of a stroke
items.cause: Pen lifted at an angle (dragging ink) or set down too heavily (pooling ink).
items.fix: Set the nib down on the paper, pause for a heartbeat, then begin the stroke. End the stroke by lifting the nib straight up, not by drawing it off the edge of the paper. A clean start and clean end are the foundation of a clean letter.

content.text: Related tutorials

Edward Johnston's Foundational hand, published in his 1906 Writing & Illuminating & Lettering, was the calligraphy alphabet that recovered the broad-edge tradition for the twentieth century. Based on the round-form Carolingian minuscules of the tenth century, with their generous proportions and clear stroke shapes, Foundational is the alphabet every modern Western calligraphy course teaches first. The reason: every other Western broad-edge hand (Italic, Uncial, Carolingian, Versals) builds on the same set of basic strokes, drawn at a constant
nib angle
against a fixed
x-height
. Practise the strokes; the alphabet follows.

Seven strokes do the work. A vertical downstroke, a horizontal across the top of the letter, the two diagonals (one for the right-leg of an n, one for the left-leg of an a), a curve forming half a circle, the dot that appears on the i and j, and the connecting hairline of an h or m. Each stroke is drawn from start to finish in a single confident movement; the nib stays at 30° to the baseline throughout. The thickness of the stroke comes from the nib's angle relative to the direction of the stroke, not from pressure on the pen.

attrs.title: Before you start
attrs.body:
Workspace. A flat surface (ideally angled at 30-45° on a drawing board if you have one) with the paper held by a small piece of masking tape at each upper corner. A piece of blotting paper or a clean cotton cloth under the writing hand stops sweat and skin oils from the writing surface. Good light at eye level.

Ink safety. Iron-gall ink stains skin and clothing semi-permanently. Wear an apron or an old shirt; keep the ink bottle on the side of the dominant hand, never in the path between the bottle and the work. Cap the bottle between uses (not just rested-on); a dip-pen working session lasts long enough that an uncapped bottle is the most common spill.

Nib safety. New nibs ship with a thin protective coating that prevents ink flow. Wipe the nib gently with a cloth before first use, OR pass it briefly through a flame (1-2 seconds) to burn off the coating. The nib's slit is sharp on a new nib; handle from the back of the nib, not the cut edge.

Eye fatigue. Calligraphy practice on a small x-height (3-4 mm) reads heavily on the eyes. Take a five-minute break every twenty minutes; look at something further than the paper to rest. A daylight task lamp helps; a yellow incandescent bulb makes ink colours read wrong and tires the eyes faster.
